bio.notes: Sarah No. 2 — replacement naming after first Sarah (H00301) who died April 1741

narrative_blocks.header: Married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt of Heath
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt and moved to Heath, Massachusetts. Leavitt was twice dismissed from churches — once for dragging a runaway enslaved woman home by rope, and once for Loyalist sympathies.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children — eleven sons and one daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She had twelve children with Jonathan Leavitt — eleven sons and one daughter named Clarissa. EH lists only three; other sources confirm the full count of twelve.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter gave her wrong medicine, killing her
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly in 1791 after her daughter Clarissa gave her the wrong medicine. Clarissa herself died unmarried seven years later — never recovering from the accident.

narrative_blocks.header: Underground Railroad station owners descended from her
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her grandsons — Roger Hooker Leavitt and Hart Leavitt — operated Underground Railroad stations in Charlemont, Massachusetts, sheltering the fugitive Basil Dorsey for five years. Roger's home is on the National Park Service Underground Railroad Network to Freedom.

narrative_blocks.header: A wrong medicine, given by her daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly at Heath on October 11, 1791, after taking the wrong medicine — handed to her, the Yale record says, by her daughter Clarissa. The funeral text was “He hath done all things well”; the Connecticut Courant carried the notice.

sources.short: Emerson Davis, Biographical Sketches of Congregational Pastors

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1758 minister at Walpole and Charlemont
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1758 and was ordained at Walpole, New Hampshire on May 27, 1761. After 1767 he served the Charlemont, Massachusetts pulpit until 1785.

narrative_blocks.header: Uncle of Chief Justice Oliver Ellsworth
narrative_blocks.body: His sister Jemima Leavitt married Capt. David Ellsworth, making him the uncle of Oliver Ellsworth — Founding Father, signer of the Constitution, and third Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court. The Ellsworth and Leavitt households were closely tied through Suffield, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Dragged a runaway enslaved woman home by rope
narrative_blocks.body: In 1764 he was dismissed from his Walpole ministry after allegedly dragging a runaway enslaved woman home behind his horse by a rope tied to his saddle. He was installed at Charlemont, Massachusetts three years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Dismissed from Charlemont for his Loyalist sentiments
narrative_blocks.body: He was dismissed from his Charlemont, Massachusetts ministry in 1785 for Loyalist sympathies during the Revolution. He preached on alone in a schoolhouse and sued his former parishioners for back wages, winning roughly £700.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died from medicine her daughter gave her
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Sarah Hooker Leavitt died in October 1791 at age 49 'as a consequence of taking wrong medicine' given to her by their daughter Clarissa. The obituary called the loss sudden and unexpected.

narrative_blocks.header: Two grandsons defended him in a 1904 book
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his grandsons John Hooker Leavitt and William Hooker Leavitt published a 1904 volume titled 'A Sketch of the Life and Character of Rev. Jonathan Leavitt.' They tried to portray their controversial ancestor as a misunderstood and complicated man.

education.notes: B.A. — Yale 1758. Tier-2 spouse of Sarah Hooker (H00302). Two brothers also Yale graduates. Nephew-by-marriage to Chief Justice Oliver Ellsworth.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, Connecticut

cross_connections.display_label: Husband Dr. Foote helped found Republican Party 1856
Husband Dr. Foote helped found Republican Party 1856

narrative_blocks.header: Husband helped organize Republican Party, 1856
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia Stiles Leavitt's second husband Dr. Elial Todd Foote — physician, judge, and New York State Assemblyman — was one of the figures who helped organize the Republican Party in 1856 at its founding convention. He was an anti-slavery activist throughout his career. Their April 27, 1841 marriage came after Amelia's widowhood from her first husband Rev. Charles Jenkins of Greenfield Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Yale President Ezra Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia's mother Emilia Stiles was a daughter of Ezra Stiles — the President of Yale College from 1778 to 1795 — making Amelia a granddaughter of one of New England's most important late-eighteenth-century theological and academic figures. Her father Hon. Jonathan Leavitt was judge and businessman of Greenfield Massachusetts, and son of Sarah Hooker, putting Amelia at the intersection of the Yale-presidential and Hooker-descent lines.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Sarah Hooker; born Greenfield 1799
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia Stiles Leavitt was a granddaughter of Sarah Hooker through her father Hon. Jonathan Leavitt — Sarah had died eight years before Amelia's 1799 birth, so they never met. Amelia herself was born December 6, 1799 in Greenfield Massachusetts and died November 26, 1867 in New Haven Connecticut, where Dr. Foote had practiced medicine.

cross_connections.display_label: was his mother, of Thomas Hooker’s blood
shared his Leavitt descent from Sarah Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: The Liberty Party’s man for Lieutenant Governor
narrative_blocks.body: President of the Franklin County Anti-Slavery Society, he accepted the new Massachusetts Liberty Party’s 1840 nomination for Lieutenant Governor — the abolitionist ticket his son Joshua’s party-building made possible. His household sheltered Basil Dorsey alongside his sons’.

narrative_blocks.header: She petitioned to free the capital
narrative_blocks.body: Chloe circulated a petition demanding the abolition of slavery in the District of Columbia while her husband ran for Lieutenant Governor on the Liberty ticket and her sons ran the Underground Railroad.

HD0867.prominent_name_reason: Joshua Leavitt (1794-1873) — abolitionist editor and publisher. Editor of The Emancipator (abolitionist newspaper) and founding editor of The Independent (influential religious newspaper, New York). Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joshua_Leavitt Son of Roger Hooker Leavitt (H00685), grandson of Sarah Hooker (H00302). First graduated Yale, then became a lawyer, then entered ministry. "Gained his greatest renown as editor of the Emancipator." Helped his brothers operate Underground Railroad stations.

cross_connections.display_label: Joshua Leavitt co-founded the American Geographical Society (INST014) in 1851.
Both central figures in the Amistad case — Leavitt organizing the legal defense, Willcox as the federal marshal who held the captives and ultimately announced their freedom.
Amistad defender from another Hooker branch — hosted Mende captives
Springfield Republican editor inheriting Bowles family abolitionist editorial legacy
sheltered the Mende his committee defended
also sat for Mathew Brady’s camera

narrative_blocks.header: He formed the Amistad defense committee
narrative_blocks.body: On September 4, 1839, Leavitt, Lewis Tappan, and Simeon Jocelyn formed the committee that raised the funds to defend the Amistad captives. His Christian Lyre of 1830 had already made him a publishing force — the first American tunebook in modern hymnal form.

narrative_blocks.header: Editor of The Emancipator from 1836
narrative_blocks.detail: Son of Roger Leavitt of Heath MA; Yale 1814; Yale Divinity 1825. He was a Congregational minister before turning to reform journalism. As editor of The Emancipator (New York, 1837-1848) he published evidence of slavery's brutality while also covering the Amistad case. He helped found the American Anti-Slavery Society with William Lloyd Garrison and served as a delegate to the World Anti-Slavery Convention in London (1840). He was also a leader in the Liberty Party and wrote extensively on the economics of slavery (The Financial Power of Slavery, 1840). His brother Roger Hooker Leavitt ran an Underground Railroad station in Montague MA. His unknowing cousin was Henry Evelyn Pierrepont (HD1533) — they co-founded the American Geographical Society in 1851 without apparently knowing they were both descendants of Thomas Hooker. (DAB; Yale; Wikipedia on Joshua Leavitt)
narrative_blocks.body: He edited The Emancipator — the flagship US abolitionist newspaper — from 1836 onward. Co-founded the New York Anti-Slavery Society in 1833 and broke from William Lloyd Garrison in 1840 to form the American and Foreign Anti-Slavery Society with Lewis Tappan.

narrative_blocks.header: Sheltered an escaped slave for over five years
narrative_blocks.body: In 1836 he directed escaped slave Basil Dorsey to his brother Roger Hooker Leavitt's Underground Railroad station at Charlemont MA. Dorsey later lived with the Leavitt family in New York City for more than five years — a major personal commitment of household resources to the antislavery cause.

narrative_blocks.header: Architect of the Liberty Party, 1844
narrative_blocks.body: He was the prime architect of the Liberty Party and ran James G. Birney for president in 1844 — contemporaries called him 'Father of the Liberty Party.' His 1840 tract 'Financial Power of Slavery' was the most-circulated piece of his political career.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded American Geographical Society, 1851
narrative_blocks.body: He co-founded the American Geographical Society in 1851 with Henry Evelyn Pierrepont. Both were 6th-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker through entirely separate branches — neither known to have been aware of their shared ancestry.

education.notes: Per Pocumtuck Valley Memorial Association history.

HD0868.prominent_name_reason: Col. Roger Hooker Leavitt — Underground Railroad operator. His home in Charlemont MA is on the National Park Service's National Underground Railroad Network to Freedom. Sheltered fugitive slave Basil Dorsey (escaped Frederick County MD 1836) for over five years. Colonel in the Massachusetts militia. Member of Massachusetts legislature. Advocate for the Hoosac Tunnel. Died in Waterloo, Iowa visiting son John Hooker Leavitt, 1885. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roger_Hooker_Leavitt Family correspondence at Library of Congress Manuscript Division.

bio.notes: Named "Hooker" honoring maternal Hooker ancestry through grandmother Sarah Hooker (H00302).

narrative_blocks.header: Basil Dorsey lived five years in hiding
narrative_blocks.body: The fugitive Basil Dorsey, escaped from Maryland in 1836, sheltered more than five years among the Leavitt households at Charlemont — hidden in plain sight while the 1840 census recorded no persons of color there. Brother Joshua wrote from New York: “do all that Christian benevolence requires in his care.”

narrative_blocks.header: The Utica mob converted his parents
narrative_blocks.body: Violence by pro-slavery factions at the October 1835 Utica convention — which he attended with brothers Joshua and Hart — fully converted the elder Leavitts to the cause. Within a year he was president of the Franklin County Anti-Slavery Society.

narrative_blocks.header: Four wives, the last at seventy-six
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed of Keziah Osgood Hunt in 1838, he married her sister Eliza the next year; Olive Warriner in Chicago at seventy-one; and at seventy-six, the schoolteacher Lucy Trowbridge — her first marriage, at fifty-one. He was also among the earliest promoters of the Hoosac Tunnel.

narrative_blocks.header: His store held the first post office
narrative_blocks.body: Relocating to Greenfield beside his uncle Judge Jonathan Leavitt’s law office, Hart kept the store that housed the town’s first newspaper and first post office — before returning to run his Charlemont farm as an Underground Railroad station.

narrative_blocks.header: “A sturdy abolitionist,” the historian wrote
narrative_blocks.body: Ohio State’s Wilbur Siebert catalogued him as an operator of the Franklin County underground network — “a sturdy abolitionist who did all he could to help slaves gain their freedom.” His farmhouse, like his brother Roger Hooker’s, stands on the NPS Network to Freedom.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the Hooker Elm's famous hospitality alive
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited the Farmington estate and the famous Hooker Elm from his father John. EH described him as 'a thorough gentleman of the old school' who continued the famed hospitality of the elm gatherings.

narrative_blocks.header: Favorite son and keeper of family history
narrative_blocks.body: He accompanied his father John on official journeys around the Colony and brought his first wife to live in the family house. EH wrote that he received from his father full information about the family's English origins — and that his father was the best informed of all Thomas Hooker's grandsons on the subject.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sisters married Hart brothers — knot tied
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah each married a son of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. Through those three marriages, Roger found himself simultaneously brother-in-law to Nathaniel, Samuel, and Matthew Hart — the Hart and Hooker families fully intertwined.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His son Thomas Hooker fathered Abigail Hooker, who married Samuel Talcott of Hartford. The marriage merged the Hooker surname into the Talcott line — a transition that defines several gen 6+ branches.

parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Bull — daughter of Thomas Bull, a Hartford Founder
parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Josiah Hart of Farmington — son of John Hart, grandson of Stephen Hart (X00034)

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.
cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law, honored in the Indian wars

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.

Both served as Connecticut Colony Supreme Court judges.
was the cousin whose Indian Neck deed he registered

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
